text:
Bacterial wound infections pose a significant global healthcare challenge due to the rapid rise of antibiotic-resistant pathogens. In response, this study introduces an innovative, antibiotic-free antimicrobial strategy utilizing a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme designed for accelerated wound healing. The composite (Fe3O4@MOF@Au NPs, or FMA NPs) was synthesized by integrating ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) into an IRMOF-3 shell that encapsulates superparamagnetic iron oxide (Fe3O4) cores. 

The FMA nanozymes demonstrate synergistically enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity, effectively catalyzing low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) to produce highly toxic hydroxyl radicals (·OH). In vitro experiments confirmed that the hybrid nanozymes possess excellent biocompatibility and potent bactericidal efficacy against both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria, including the successful disruption of established biofilms. In vivo assessments using a mouse model of infected wounds further revealed that FMA NPs significantly promote tissue repair and re-epithelialization with high biosafety and no systemic toxicity. These results highlight the potential of FMA hybrid nanozymes as a robust and biocompatible platform for the clinical management of bacterial infections and wound care without the need for traditional antibiotics.
